heroOverview: Agnosia is a neurological condition in which a person cannot recognise or identify objects, people, sounds, or smells despite having intact sensory organs and preserved intelligence. The deficit lies not in the sensory apparatus — vision, hearing, or touch are essentially normal — but in the higher-order cortical processes that assign meaning to incoming sensory signals. Visual agnosia, the most clinically recognised subtype, typically follows bilateral or right-hemisphere occipitotemporal lesions, while auditory agnosia follows superior temporal damage and tactile agnosia follows parietal lesions. Stroke is the most common cause, but brain tumours, traumatic injury, carbon monoxide poisoning, and neurodegenerative diseases also produce it. The condition profoundly disrupts daily function, making even familiar faces, everyday objects, or ambient sounds unrecognisable, yet the same information remains perfectly identifiable through another sensory channel.

keyStats.prevalence: Agnosia is considered a rare clinical syndrome; precise population figures are unavailable but it is estimated to affect fewer than 1 in 10,000 adults as an isolated diagnosis, though perceptual recognition difficulties occur in a substantial proportion of stroke survivors
keyStats.demographics: Most cases occur in adults over 50 following stroke or brain injury; no strong sex predilection
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically corresponds to the age of peak stroke incidence, concentrated in the 60–80 age range
keyStats.globalCases: Global prevalence data are not available; agnosia appears in approximately 1–2% of acute stroke admissions with significant hemispheric lesions

definition: Agnosia (ICD-10: R48.1) is a failure of recognition in one or more sensory modalities that cannot be explained by sensory impairment, intellectual deterioration, inattention, or language dysfunction. The term — from the Greek 'a-gnosis' (without knowledge) — was introduced by Sigmund Freud in 1891 and systematically elaborated by neurologist Heinrich Lissauer, who distinguished between apperceptive agnosia (failure to form an accurate percept of the stimulus) and associative agnosia (accurate percept formed but meaning cannot be retrieved). Visual agnosia is the best-characterised subtype, arising from damage to the ventral visual processing stream in the occipital and inferior temporal lobes. Prosopagnosia (inability to recognise familiar faces) is a specific and relatively common variant, present in a developmental form in roughly 2% of the population and in an acquired form following right fusiform gyrus damage. Auditory agnosia involves inability to identify sounds despite normal audiometry; when speech sounds specifically cannot be recognised, the term pure word deafness is used. Tactile agnosia (astereognosis) involves failure to identify objects by touch with intact tactile sensation. Simultanagnosia — inability to perceive more than one object at a time despite recognising each individually — is a component of Balint syndrome following bilateral parieto-occipital lesions. In clinical practice, agnosia rarely occurs in pure isolation and is usually part of a broader neurological syndrome following hemispheric injury.

typesClassification.description: Failure to form an accurate internal representation of a visual object; the person cannot copy or match shapes they cannot name. Caused by diffuse occipital damage, often from carbon monoxide poisoning or anoxia.

The percept is formed normally — objects can be copied or matched — but meaning cannot be retrieved. The person draws what they see but cannot name it or describe its function. Caused by bilateral occipitotemporal lesions.

Selective inability to recognise familiar faces including, in severe cases, one's own face in a mirror. Other visual recognition (objects, words) is typically preserved. Follows right or bilateral fusiform gyrus damage.
Failure to recognise environmental sounds (dog bark, telephone ring) with normal pure-tone audiometry. Pure word deafness is a subtype in which specifically spoken words cannot be recognised.
Inability to identify objects placed in the hand by touch alone, with intact touch and proprioception when tested separately. Follows contralateral parietal lesions.

Inability to perceive more than one object or scene element simultaneously; a component of Balint syndrome caused by bilateral parieto-occipital lesions; patients can identify each item but cannot perceive the whole scene.

primarySymptoms: Inability to recognise familiar objects by sight — picking up a key and being unable to name or demonstrate its use, though it is recognised immediately when placed in the hand.
Failure to recognise familiar faces (prosopagnosia) — including close family members, long-term friends, or one's own reflection, despite being able to describe facial features accurately.
Inability to identify familiar environmental sounds (auditory agnosia) — a dog barking or a phone ringing sounds 'like noise' and cannot be identified.
Failure to identify objects by touch alone (tactile agnosia) — an object in the closed hand cannot be named, but vision of the same object immediately identifies it.
Severe difficulty recognising written words (visual word form agnosia) — letters can be traced individually but their combination as a word conveys no meaning.
Inability to process more than one visual object at a time (simultanagnosia) — describing a picture by listing individual elements but unable to see the scene as a whole.
Preserved sensory acuity — visual, auditory, or tactile testing in isolation is normal or near-normal, demonstrating that the impairment is perceptual-cognitive rather than sensory.
Cross-modal recognition intact — an object not identified by sight is immediately recognised when touched, heard, or described verbally.
Difficulty navigating familiar environments (topographical agnosia) — landmarks that were once instantly recognisable appear unfamiliar.
Preserved naming when prompted — when an agnosic patient is told what an object is, they can confirm recognition, distinguishing agnosia from anomia.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing difficulty recognising acquaintances' faces at meetings or social events, attributed to poor memory rather than perception.
Needing to hear a sound's context (e.g. 'the dog is barking') to identify it, rather than recognising it directly.
Handling objects for longer than usual before being able to use them, as if needing time to figure out what they are.
Becoming disoriented in familiar environments, unable to use visual landmarks.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of failure to recognise objects, people, or surroundings — requires immediate emergency evaluation as a possible stroke.
Sudden perceptual changes accompanied by weakness, facial droop, or speech difficulty — call 999/911 immediately.
Acute agnosia in the context of severe headache, fever, or reduced consciousness, suggesting encephalitis or haemorrhage.
Rapid bilateral visual deterioration with perceptual failures suggesting posterior circulation stroke.

causes.description: Infarction of the occipitotemporal cortex — particularly the fusiform gyrus and lingual gyrus — or parietal cortex causes the most common acquired agnosias; the posterior cerebral artery territory is most often involved in visual and prosopagnosic presentations.
Intracerebral haemorrhage in occipital, temporal, or parietal regions produces the same recognition deficits as ischaemic infarction; recovery may be more complete as haematoma resolves.

Closed head injury producing contusions or diffuse axonal injury in occipitotemporal and parietal areas causes agnosia; penetrating wounds to visual association cortex are an established cause in military populations.

Carbon monoxide poisoning selectively damages the boundary zones between watershed areas of the occipital cortex, typically producing apperceptive visual agnosia; cardiac arrest and severe hypoxia cause similar cortical boundary-zone damage.

Posterior cortical atrophy — a variant of Alzheimer's disease — begins with progressive visual recognition failure, including object and face recognition loss, before memory and language are prominently affected; corticobasal degeneration also causes parietal agnosia.

Gliomas or metastases in occipitotemporal regions, and resection of temporal or occipital tumours, can produce persistent agnosia; radiation necrosis of posterior cortex is an occasional delayed cause.

riskFactors.description: Stroke incidence rises steeply with age; the majority of acquired agnosia cases follow posterior circulation or MCA territory strokes in older adults.
Sustained hypertension is the primary modifiable driver of both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke, the most common cause of acquired agnosia.
Cardioembolic strokes from AF frequently affect the posterior circulation supplying occipital and temporal cortex; anticoagulation is the most effective preventive intervention.
Diabetes doubles stroke risk through accelerated large- and small-vessel disease and increases the probability of posterior circulation involvement.
Smoking roughly doubles stroke risk including posterior circulation strokes; cessation substantially reduces the risk within 2–5 years.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational or recreational carbon monoxide exposure
riskFactors.description: Poorly ventilated settings with gas appliances, generators, or combustion engines pose exposure risk; carbon monoxide alarms and adequate ventilation are effective prevention.
APOE ε4 allele and MAPT mutations increase risk of Alzheimer's disease and frontotemporal degeneration respectively, which can present with agnosia as a prominent early feature.
A history of moderate-to-severe TBI causing occipital or temporal contusion is a direct risk for persistent visual or auditory agnosia.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 following posterior circulation or posterior MCA territory stroke
Adults of any age following severe traumatic brain injury with occipitotemporal involvement
Adults following carbon monoxide poisoning or anoxic brain injury
Adults aged 50–70 with posterior cortical atrophy (Alzheimer variant)
People with developmental prosopagnosia — estimated at roughly 2% of the general population
Men and women approximately equally affected in acquired forms
diagnosisOverview: Agnosia is primarily a clinical diagnosis, established by demonstrating intact sensory function alongside a specific failure of recognition. The evaluation begins with a standard history clarifying onset (sudden vs. gradual), modality affected, and whether recognition is restored through another sense. Sensory testing excludes visual acuity loss, hearing impairment, or peripheral neuropathy as the cause. Visual agnosia is assessed with confrontation tasks: object naming from sight, drawing to command versus copying, and matching tasks. Prosopagnosia is evaluated with famous-face recognition tests and immediate-memory facial identification. Auditory agnosia assessment requires pure-tone audiometry to confirm normal hearing followed by environmental sound recognition tasks. Tactile agnosia is assessed by placing objects in the hand while excluding vision. Neuroimaging is mandatory: MRI with T1, FLAIR, and DWI sequences defines lesion location and extent; functional MRI or PET identifies residual metabolic activity in affected regions. Neuropsychological assessment characterises the full cognitive profile and distinguishes agnosia from anomia (where providing a verbal description allows naming), dementia (where global cognitive decline accompanies the recognition failure), and neglect (where hemispherically lateralised inattention is the primary mechanism). The key diagnostic principle is modality specificity — recognising objects through one sense while failing through another confirms that the deficit is truly agnosic rather than sensory.

diagnosticTests.test: Object and face recognition battery (clinical assessment)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes which sensory modality and recognition category are impaired, confirming the agnosia diagnosis and guiding lesion localisation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A neuropsychologist or neurologist presents objects, photographs, or sounds and asks you to name or identify them; the session takes 30–60 minutes and is entirely non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.test: Basic sensory examination (visual acuity, audiometry, tactile testing)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes peripheral sensory impairment as the cause of recognition failure — a critical prerequisite for diagnosing agnosia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Includes reading an eye chart, tones through headphones (audiometry), and light touch or vibration applied to the hands; all are painless.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with DWI, FLAIR, and T1 sequences
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the location, extent, and aetiology of the causative lesion (infarct, haemorrhage, atrophy, or tumour) in occipitotemporal, parietal, or temporal regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scan takes 30–45 minutes; involves loud knocking sounds; contrast may be given to identify tumour or inflammatory pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps regional glucose metabolism, useful for identifying posterior cortical atrophy patterns and characterising neurodegenerative agnosia when structural MRI is relatively normal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small radiotracer dose is injected and scanning takes 30–45 minutes; low-dose radiation is involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises all cognitive domains to distinguish pure agnosia from broader dementia, establishes baseline severity, and guides rehabilitation planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 1–2 hour session with written and verbal tasks covering memory, attention, language, and perception.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes epileptiform activity as a cause of episodic perceptual disturbance that could mimic agnosia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrodes are placed on the scalp and brain electrical activity is recorded over 20–40 minutes; painless.

treatmentOverview: No pharmacological treatment reverses agnosia directly; management centres on treating the underlying cause, compensatory rehabilitation, and environmental adaptation. When agnosia results from acute ischaemic stroke, emergency thrombolysis or thrombectomy directed at posterior circulation occlusion can limit cortical damage and improve recognition outcomes, making immediate hospital attendance critical. Rehabilitation is led by an occupational therapist and neuropsychologist working collaboratively. Compensation strategies are the primary rehabilitation approach: because agnosia is modality-specific, retraining intact alternative sense channels — using sound or touch to identify objects that vision no longer identifies, labelling household items, using voice-ID notifications for faces — restores significant functional independence. Specific perceptual training — repeated presentation of stimuli with feedback in blocked then randomised formats — can produce measurable gains in recognition, particularly for visual object agnosia and prosopagnosia. Environmental modifications (consistent object placement, distinctive tactile cues, named photographs of family members placed prominently) are highly practical. For prosopagnosia, compensatory strategies using voice recognition, hairstyle, or gait cues are effective. Technology assists: face-recognition apps on smartphones can substitute for impaired prosopagnosia. Treating co-existing post-stroke depression optimises engagement with rehabilitation. For neurodegenerative causes, cholinesterase inhibitors used for underlying Alzheimer's disease may modestly slow the progression of perceptual decline.

medicalTreatments.name: Acute stroke reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis and thrombectomy)
medicalTreatments.description: In acute posterior circulation or hemispheric stroke causing sudden agnosia, IV alteplase within 4.5 hours and thrombectomy within an appropriate window can restore perfusion to visual association cortex and limit agnosia severity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mechanical thrombectomy for posterior circulation occlusion associated with approximately 40–50% achieving good functional outcome versus 20–30% without intervention (pooled posterior circulation trial data).

medicalTreatments.name: Occupational therapy with perceptual rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: Systematic practice of recognition tasks using hierarchical cueing (from intact to impaired modality), errorless learning, and strategy training to develop compensatory identification approaches for daily objects and faces.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured perceptual training shows improvement in 60–70% of participants with mild-to-moderate visual agnosia in case series and small RCTs; generalisation to daily function improves with ecologically valid training.

medicalTreatments.name: Neuropsychological strategy training
medicalTreatments.description: Explicit teaching of non-visual (tactile, auditory, contextual) identification strategies and self-monitoring techniques allowing functional independence despite persistent visual recognition failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Compensatory strategy programmes demonstrate functional independence gains in approximately 50–65% of patients with visual agnosia in rehabilitation settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Cholinesterase inhibitors (for Alzheimer-type posterior cortical atrophy)
medicalTreatments.description: Donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine prescribed for the underlying Alzheimer pathology in posterior cortical atrophy; may modestly slow rate of perceptual and cognitive decline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase II trials suggest approximately 2–3 point improvement on MMSE-equivalent scales at 12 months versus placebo, with no direct agnosia-specific outcome data.

medicalTreatments.name: Secondary stroke prevention pharmacotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Antiplatelet agents, anticoagulation for AF, statins, and antihypertensives preventing recurrent stroke that would worsen or re-trigger recognition deficits.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antiplatelet plus statin plus antihypertensive combination reduces 5-year recurrent stroke risk by approximately 50% compared to no secondary prevention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Face-recognition technology and smartphone aids
alternativeTreatments.description: Mobile apps using real-time face-detection and naming assist people with prosopagnosia in identifying colleagues, family, and public figures in real time, substantially increasing social independence.

alternativeTreatments.name: Environmental labelling and cueing systems
alternativeTreatments.description: Tactile labels (Braille-like or textured), colour coding, and systematic object placement transform the environment so that non-visual cues substitute for visual recognition.

alternativeTreatments.name: Peer support and psychological counselling
alternativeTreatments.description: Living with face or object blindness carries significant psychological burden; contact with others who have prosopagnosia or acquired agnosia (through organisations like the Prosopagnosia Research Centre) normalises the experience and shares practical strategies.

whySeeSpecialist: Agnosia requires a neurologist to establish the aetiology — distinguishing acute vascular, traumatic, or neurodegenerative causes is essential for targeted treatment and prognosis. A neuropsychologist provides formal characterisation of the recognition deficits and guides rehabilitation strategy. An occupational therapist translates neuropsychological findings into practical daily-living adaptations. For prosopagnosia or posterior cortical atrophy, a cognitive neurologist with expertise in visual agnosia syndromes provides the most accurate diagnostic and prognostic information. Delayed or missed diagnosis leads to unnecessary restrictions (e.g., loss of driving licence based on presumed visual loss) and delayed rehabilitation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For sudden-onset agnosia, attend the nearest emergency department immediately for stroke evaluation. For subacute or chronic presentations, seek a neurologist with interest in visual-perceptual or cognitive disorders; academic medical centres and specialist memory and perception clinics offer the most experienced multidisciplinary teams. Ask specifically about access to neuropsychological testing, perceptual rehabilitation, and occupational therapy with environmental assessment. For suspected posterior cortical atrophy, referral to a dementia specialist centre provides both diagnostic imaging and coordinated long-term management.
preventionStrategies: Control hypertension and atrial fibrillation — the two most powerful modifiable risk factors for the posterior circulation strokes that most commonly cause agnosia.
Quit smoking to halve stroke risk over 2–5 years.
Install carbon monoxide detectors and ensure adequate ventilation of gas appliances — prevents the anoxic occipital injury causing apperceptive agnosia.
Use helmets and head protection during contact sports and cycling to reduce risk of occipital and temporal TBI.
Manage diabetes and hyperlipidaemia with appropriate pharmacotherapy.
Attend emergency services immediately for any sudden change in visual recognition or perceptual function.
lifestyleModifications: Establish fixed locations for commonly used objects at home so tactile and spatial memory substitute for visual recognition.
Label personal items, food containers, and medications with large-print, tactile, or colour-coded tags.
Use voice-based assistants (smart speakers) and text-to-speech tools to access information without relying on visual recognition.
Inform family, friends, and colleagues about the condition and effective communication strategies.
Avoid driving until formally assessed by a specialist driving rehabilitation service — agnosia may impair safe hazard recognition.
Maintain social connections through structured activities where familiar voices and routines compensate for face-recognition failure.

recommended: Mediterranean or DASH diet pattern for cardiovascular and cerebrovascular risk reduction
Foods rich in B vitamins (folate, B6, B12) supporting homocysteine metabolism and reducing vascular risk
Antioxidant-rich vegetables and fruits supporting neurovascular health
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 fatty acids
avoid: High-sodium processed foods that elevate blood pressure
Saturated and trans fats contributing to dyslipidaemia
Excess calories leading to obesity and metabolic syndrome

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged for cardiovascular risk reduction and to support cerebral plasticity relevant to perceptual recovery. The AHA recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity weekly. Exercise programmes for agnosia patients should be supervised until the safety of navigating unfamiliar environments is established, given that topographical disorientation and visual perceptual deficits may create hazards. Group exercise in familiar settings, guided by an occupational therapist for environmental adaptation, is preferable in the early recovery phase.
dailyManagement: Use systematic object placement routines — return items to fixed locations after every use.
Adopt a daily structured routine that reduces the need for real-time visual identification decisions.
Carry a communication card explaining the condition for use in public or healthcare settings.
Take all secondary stroke-prevention medications consistently.
Attend follow-up neuropsychology and occupational therapy appointments to update compensatory strategies as recovery evolves.
Report any new or worsening perceptual changes immediately, as they may signal recurrent stroke.
prognosis: Prognosis depends strongly on aetiology, lesion size, and age. Agnosia following small posterior circulation strokes often improves substantially over weeks to months as perilesional oedema resolves and cortical reorganisation occurs; partial or complete recovery of object recognition is documented in approximately 50–60% of patients with limited lesions. Agnosia from large bilateral occipitotemporal infarction or anoxic injury tends to be more persistent, particularly apperceptive agnosia. Prosopagnosia following unilateral right fusiform lesion has variable recovery; developmental prosopagnosia is lifelong but compatible with normal function through compensation. Posterior cortical atrophy (Alzheimer variant) follows an inexorably progressive course over 8–12 years from onset, with perceptual deficits eventually joined by memory and executive impairment. Occupational therapy and neuropsychological rehabilitation substantially improve functional independence regardless of anatomical recovery.
recoveryTimeline: Spontaneous improvement in acute stroke-related agnosia is fastest in the first 8–12 weeks as cerebral oedema resolves. Therapy-driven improvement continues through the first 6–12 months; compensatory strategy gains extend beyond this period. By 12 months, the level of residual deficit is largely established anatomically, though continued rehabilitation refines strategies and supports adaptation. For traumatic injury in younger adults, recovery windows may extend further due to greater neuroplasticity.
complications: Road traffic accidents and domestic injuries from failure to recognise hazards, requiring driving assessment and home safety review.
Social isolation and depression from inability to recognise faces, estimated to affect approximately 25–40% of patients with significant prosopagnosia.
Diagnostic delay and misattribution — agnosia is frequently mistaken for memory loss, confusion, or psychiatric presentation, delaying appropriate rehabilitation.
Medication errors — visual agnosia may prevent correct identification of pill bottles and blister packs, requiring pharmacist labelling adaptations.
Reduced vocational function — recognition deficits impair most occupational roles, with return to previous employment uncommon without formal workplace adaptation.
Caregiver burden — supervising daily activities for a person with significant agnosia requires substantial time investment from family members.

supportResources.name: Prosopagnosia Research at Dartmouth and Harvard
supportResources.description: A research and support resource for people with face blindness (prosopagnosia), offering self-assessment tools and information on current research.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Agnosia Information Page
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of agnosia from the U.S. National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.

supportResources.name: Headway — Brain injury charity (UK)
supportResources.description: UK-based support for people with all forms of acquired brain injury including those with perceptual disorders such as agnosia.

sources.title: Farah MJ. Visual Agnosia, 2nd ed. MIT Press, 2004 (review of clinical and experimental literature)
Riddoch MJ, Humphreys GW. Visual agnosia. Neurol Clin 2003
Duchaine BC, Nakayama K. Developmental prosopagnosia: a window to content-specific face processing. Curr Opin Neurobiol 2006
NICE NG128 — Stroke and transient ischaemic attack in over 16s: diagnosis and initial management, 2019
Crutch SJ et al. Posterior cortical atrophy. Lancet Neurol 2012
WHO — Neurological disorders public health challenges, 2006

confusedWithConditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alzheimer's disease causes global cognitive decline including memory loss; agnosia in isolation preserves memory and intelligence, though posterior cortical atrophy — an Alzheimer variant — can present primarily as visual agnosia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neurological neglect syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hemispatial neglect involves failure to attend to stimuli on one side of space due to attentional dysfunction; agnosia is a bilateral failure of recognition despite intact attention to the stimulus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Visual agnosia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Visual agnosia (R48.3) is coded separately as a subtype; the broader agnosia code (R48.1) encompasses all modalities. Both share the same mechanism of recognition failure above the level of primary sensation.

relatedConditions.name: Visual agnosia
relatedConditions.relevance: Visual agnosia is the most common subtype of agnosia, coded separately; both share the same pathophysiological mechanism and rehabilitation approach.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Stroke is the most common cause of acquired agnosia; vascular risk management and secondary prevention are directly relevant.

relatedConditions.name: Apraxia
relatedConditions.relevance: Apraxia and agnosia frequently co-occur after parieto-occipital strokes and together represent the hallmarks of Balint syndrome and posterior cortical atrophy.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Posterior cortical atrophy, an Alzheimer variant, presents primarily as progressive agnosia and is managed within the Alzheimer's disease framework.

faqs.question: What is agnosia?
faqs.answer: Agnosia is the inability to recognise objects, people, or sounds despite normal sensory organs. The fault is in brain processing, not in the eyes, ears, or hands. For example, a person with visual agnosia sees clearly but cannot identify what they are looking at.

faqs.question: What causes agnosia?
faqs.answer: Stroke is the most common cause, typically damaging occipitotemporal or parietal cortex. Traumatic brain injury, carbon monoxide poisoning, brain tumours, and neurodegenerative diseases such as posterior cortical atrophy also cause it.

faqs.question: What are the types of agnosia?
faqs.answer: The main types are visual agnosia (cannot recognise objects by sight), prosopagnosia (cannot recognise faces), auditory agnosia (cannot identify sounds), and tactile agnosia (cannot identify objects by touch). Each reflects damage to a different brain region.

faqs.question: What is prosopagnosia?
faqs.answer: Prosopagnosia is the inability to recognise familiar faces, including close family members or one's own reflection. It can be acquired after brain injury to the right fusiform gyrus, or developmental — present from childhood in roughly 2% of people — without any brain damage.

faqs.question: How is agnosia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A clinician confirms that sensory function is intact (normal vision, hearing, or touch) and then demonstrates failure to recognise objects through that sense. Brain MRI identifies the causative lesion. Neuropsychological testing characterises the full extent of the recognition deficit.

faqs.question: Can agnosia be treated?
faqs.answer: There is no drug that directly reverses agnosia. Treatment focuses on rehabilitating intact senses — using touch or sound to identify what vision fails to — and adapting the environment. For stroke-caused agnosia, emergency reperfusion therapy limits initial damage.

faqs.question: Is agnosia permanent?
faqs.answer: Not always. When caused by a small stroke or transient injury, recovery of recognition can be substantial over weeks to months. Large bilateral lesions, anoxic injury, or neurodegenerative causes tend to produce more persistent deficits. Compensation strategies improve function regardless.

faqs.question: What is the difference between agnosia and amnesia?
faqs.answer: Amnesia is a memory disorder; agnosia is a recognition disorder. A person with amnesia forgets events or fails to learn new information. A person with agnosia can remember what a cat is and describe it in words but cannot recognise a cat when they see one.

faqs.question: Can someone with agnosia live independently?
faqs.answer: Many people with agnosia achieve substantial independence with compensatory strategies — labelling objects, using tactile cues, leveraging intact senses, and modifying their environment. Severity varies widely; mild prosopagnosia may need only minor adjustments while severe visual agnosia requires significant support.

faqs.question: Does agnosia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: No. Agnosia specifically impairs recognition in one or more sensory modalities while leaving intelligence, memory, and other cognitive functions intact. This is a defining diagnostic criterion — if broader cognitive decline is present, alternative diagnoses such as dementia are considered.

faqs.question: What is auditory agnosia?
faqs.answer: Auditory agnosia is the inability to identify sounds — such as a doorbell or dog barking — despite normal hearing on audiometry. Pure word deafness is a subtype in which specifically spoken words cannot be understood. It follows damage to the bilateral superior temporal cortex.

faqs.question: Can children have agnosia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Developmental prosopagnosia affects roughly 2% of children without any brain damage. Acquired agnosia in children can follow encephalitis, TBI, or posterior circulation stroke; outcomes are often better than in adults due to greater brain plasticity.

faqs.question: Is face blindness a form of agnosia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prosopagnosia — commonly called face blindness — is a subtype of visual agnosia that specifically affects face recognition. It can be acquired after right fusiform gyrus damage or developmental in origin, and affects approximately 2–2.5% of the general population in the developmental form.

faqs.question: When should I seek emergency care for agnosia?
faqs.answer: Sudden onset of failure to recognise objects, people, or places requires immediate emergency assessment as a possible stroke. Call 999 or 911 immediately. Do not wait to see if it improves — the window for stroke treatment is narrow.

faqs.question: Can someone with agnosia drive safely?
faqs.answer: Driving should be stopped immediately at diagnosis and resumed only after a formal assessment by a specialist driving rehabilitation service confirms safe hazard recognition. Visual agnosia in particular creates significant road safety risks and is typically incompatible with driving in severe cases.

faqs.question: What is tactile agnosia?
faqs.answer: Tactile agnosia, also called astereognosis, is the inability to identify objects placed in the hand by touch alone, despite normal touch and pressure sensation when tested separately. It follows damage to the contralateral parietal somatosensory association cortex.

faqs.question: What is simultanagnosia?
faqs.answer: Simultanagnosia is the inability to perceive more than one object or detail at a time. A person can identify each element of a scene individually but cannot perceive the scene as a whole. It is part of Balint syndrome, caused by bilateral parieto-occipital damage.

faqs.question: Does agnosia affect reading?
faqs.answer: Visual agnosia can include a visual word form component (pure alexia) in which letters or words cannot be visually recognised. Auditory agnosia for speech (pure word deafness) prevents understanding of spoken words. Both require specialised rehabilitation approaches within the broader agnosia management framework.

metaDescription: Agnosia is the inability to recognise objects, faces or sounds despite normal senses. Learn causes, types, diagnosis and rehabilitation options. Doctor-reviewed.
